title: Capitalization & Punctuation Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: Capital letters and punctuation marks (commas, periods, apostrophes) follow specific rules in English, and getting them wrong can make writing look careless or even change the meaning of a sentence. Here are 50 common mistakes with capitalization and punctuation, explained simply, with the correct rule to apply.

commonMistakes.wrong: i am going to the market.
commonMistakes.right: I am going to the market.
commonMistakes.why: The pronoun 'I' is always capitalized in English, no matter where it appears in the sentence - this is a unique rule specific to this one word. Always capitalize 'I' whether it's at the start, middle, or end of a sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: my friend is from india.
commonMistakes.right: My friend is from India.
commonMistakes.why: Two capitalization rules are missing here: the first word of a sentence must be capitalized, and proper nouns like country names (India) are always capitalized. Check both the start of every sentence and every proper noun (names of people, places, days, months) for correct capitalization.

commonMistakes.wrong: she was born in march.
commonMistakes.right: She was born in March.
commonMistakes.why: Month names are always capitalized in English, along with the first word of the sentence. Remember: days of the week and months of the year are always proper nouns, requiring capital letters.

commonMistakes.wrong: we celebrate diwali every year.
commonMistakes.right: We celebrate Diwali every year.
commonMistakes.why: Names of festivals and holidays (Diwali, Christmas, Eid) are proper nouns and need capital letters. Treat festival names the same way you'd treat any other proper noun - always capitalized.

commonMistakes.wrong: he speaks english and hindi.
commonMistakes.right: He speaks English and Hindi.
commonMistakes.why: Language names are always capitalized in English, since they're derived from proper nouns (countries or regions). Remember: English, Hindi, Spanish, French - all languages get capital letters, even mid-sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: whats your name
commonMistakes.right: What's your name?
commonMistakes.why: Two things are missing: the apostrophe in the contraction "what's" (what is), and the question mark at the end since this is a question. Always check contractions for their apostrophe, and questions for their question mark.

commonMistakes.wrong: im going home now
commonMistakes.right: I'm going home now.
commonMistakes.why: This is missing the apostrophe in "I'm" (I am) and the period at the end of the sentence. Contractions always need an apostrophe to show where letters have been removed.

commonMistakes.wrong: dont forget to call me
commonMistakes.right: Don't forget to call me.
commonMistakes.why: The contraction "don't" (do not) needs an apostrophe between the n and t, and the sentence needs a capital letter at the start and a period at the end. Always place the apostrophe exactly where letters are removed - in 'don't', it replaces the missing 'o' from 'not'.

commonMistakes.wrong: its a beautiful day today
commonMistakes.right: It's a beautiful day today.
commonMistakes.why: "It's" (meaning 'it is') needs an apostrophe, and the sentence needs both a capital letter at the start and a period at the end. This is one of the most common punctuation mistakes, so it's worth double-checking every time you use 'its' or "it's".

commonMistakes.wrong: the meeting is at 3pm on monday
commonMistakes.right: The meeting is at 3 PM on Monday.
commonMistakes.why: This is missing capitalization for the sentence start and the day name 'Monday', and it's good practice to add a space before 'PM' with capital letters. Days of the week always need capital letters, just like months.

commonMistakes.wrong: i think, therefore i am.
commonMistakes.right: I think, therefore, I am.
commonMistakes.why: Beyond capitalizing 'I' both times, this famous phrase traditionally includes a comma after 'therefore' as well, to properly set off the transitional word. Transitional words like 'therefore', 'however', and 'moreover' are often set off with commas on both sides when placed mid-sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: lets eat grandma
commonMistakes.right: Let's eat, Grandma!
commonMistakes.why: This famous example shows how missing punctuation can change meaning entirely - without a comma, it sounds like you want to eat your grandmother, but with the comma, you're addressing her about eating. Commas before a name when speaking directly to someone (called direct address) are essential for clarity and can even be a matter of life and death, as this joke illustrates!

commonMistakes.wrong: i have a dog a cat and a bird.
commonMistakes.right: I have a dog, a cat, and a bird.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence needs commas between each item in the list to separate them clearly - without commas, the items run together confusingly. Always separate three or more items in a list with commas, including before 'and' (called the Oxford comma, though its use varies by style).

commonMistakes.wrong: she said 'I will be there soon'
commonMistakes.right: She said, "I will be there soon."
commonMistakes.why: In American English, double quotation marks are standard for direct speech (not single quotes), and a comma is needed before the quote begins, plus a period inside the closing quotation mark. Use double quotes for speech in American English, and place the closing punctuation inside the quotation marks.

commonMistakes.wrong: the report is due on friday, however, i wont be able to submit it on time.
commonMistakes.right: The report is due on Friday. However, I won't be able to submit it on time.
commonMistakes.why: This is actually a comma splice mistake - 'however' used this way needs a period (or semicolon) before it, not just a comma, since it's joining two complete sentences. Remember: 'however' as a transition word between two full sentences needs a period or semicolon before it, not a comma alone.

commonMistakes.wrong: my brothers name is raj.
commonMistakes.right: My brother's name is Raj.
commonMistakes.why: This needs an apostrophe to show possession ('brother's' meaning belonging to the brother) and a capital letter for the proper noun 'Raj'. Remember: apostrophe + s shows singular possession (brother's), and names always need capital letters.

commonMistakes.wrong: the childrens toys were scattered everywhere.
commonMistakes.right: The children's toys were scattered everywhere.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'children' is already plural (without adding 's'), the possessive form simply adds apostrophe + s ('children's'), following the irregular plural possessive rule. For irregular plurals (children, men, women), add apostrophe + s, just like a singular noun would.

commonMistakes.wrong: the dogs bone was buried in the yard.
commonMistakes.right: The dog's bone was buried in the yard.
commonMistakes.why: This needs an apostrophe before the 's' to show the bone belongs to one dog ('dog's'), following the standard singular possessive rule. Remember: for singular nouns, add apostrophe before the s to show possession.

commonMistakes.wrong: the students papers were graded quickly.
commonMistakes.right: The students' papers were graded quickly.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'students' is already plural (ending in s), the possessive apostrophe goes AFTER the s ('students''), not before it. For regular plural nouns already ending in s, add just an apostrophe after the s, not another 's'.

commonMistakes.wrong: James book is on the table.
commonMistakes.right: James's book is on the table. / James' book is on the table.
commonMistakes.why: For singular names ending in 's' (like James), both 'James's' and 'James'' are considered acceptable in different style guides - choose one and be consistent. Either style works, but pick one approach and use it consistently throughout your writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: she asked me, "where are you going"
commonMistakes.right: She asked me, "Where are you going?"
commonMistakes.why: The quoted question needs its own question mark inside the quotation marks, and the first word of the quote should be capitalized since it starts a new sentence within the quote. Always capitalize the first word of a quoted sentence, and include appropriate end punctuation inside the quotes.

commonMistakes.wrong: he lives on Main street.
commonMistakes.right: He lives on Main Street.
commonMistakes.why: When 'Street' is part of an official place name, it should be capitalized just like the rest of the name (Main Street). Capitalize words like Street, Avenue, and Road when they're part of a specific, named location.

commonMistakes.wrong: she works at the Company down the road.
commonMistakes.right: She works at the company down the road.
commonMistakes.why: Unless referring to a specific, named company, 'company' here is a common noun and shouldn't be capitalized. Only capitalize words like 'company', 'college', or 'university' when they're part of an official proper name (like Apple Company or Delhi University).

commonMistakes.wrong: we visited the taj mahal last summer.
commonMistakes.right: We visited the Taj Mahal last summer.
commonMistakes.why: Famous landmark names are proper nouns and need capital letters for every important word in the name. Treat multi-word landmark and place names the same way: capitalize each significant word.

commonMistakes.wrong: my Mom is a doctor.
commonMistakes.right: My mom is a doctor. / My Mom is a doctor (if used as a name/title directly).
commonMistakes.why: When 'mom' is used generally (my mom, his mom), it's a common noun and stays lowercase - but when used directly as a name in place of 'Mom' (like 'Hi, Mom!'), it's capitalized. Check whether you're using a family term generally (lowercase) or as a direct name/title (capitalized).

commonMistakes.wrong: Can you help me, please.
commonMistakes.right: Can you help me, please?
commonMistakes.why: Since this sentence is a question (asking for help), it needs a question mark at the end, not a period. Always check whether a sentence is truly a question and needs a question mark, even if it sounds like a polite request.

commonMistakes.wrong: That's amazing.!
commonMistakes.right: That's amazing!
commonMistakes.why: This sentence has both a period and an exclamation mark together, which is unnecessary - only one end punctuation mark is needed per sentence. Choose one final punctuation mark: period, question mark, or exclamation mark, never combine them.

commonMistakes.wrong: Why did you do that?!
commonMistakes.right: Why did you do that?! (acceptable informally) / Why did you do that?
commonMistakes.why: While combining '?!' is common in very casual writing to show shocked surprise, formal writing should use just one punctuation mark (usually the question mark alone). Save combined punctuation like '?!' for informal contexts; use single end punctuation in formal writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: The company's, revenue increased this year.
commonMistakes.right: The company's revenue increased this year.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence has an unnecessary comma right after the possessive apostrophe-s - possessives don't need a comma immediately following them. Remove commas that don't serve a clear grammatical purpose, like separating necessary information.

commonMistakes.wrong: My sister who lives in Mumbai, is visiting us.
commonMistakes.right: My sister, who lives in Mumbai, is visiting us.
commonMistakes.why: This needs a comma BEFORE 'who' as well, since 'who lives in Mumbai' is extra, non-essential information about the sister (called a non-restrictive clause) and needs commas on both sides. Non-essential descriptive clauses need commas both before and after, not just after.

commonMistakes.wrong: The book, that I borrowed from the library, is due tomorrow.
commonMistakes.right: The book that I borrowed from the library is due tomorrow.
commonMistakes.why: Unlike the previous example, this clause ('that I borrowed from the library') is ESSENTIAL to identifying which book, so it shouldn't have commas around it at all. The key difference: essential/restrictive clauses (usually with 'that') don't get commas; non-essential/non-restrictive clauses (usually with 'which' or 'who') do.

commonMistakes.wrong: Please bring your laptop, phone, and charger, to the meeting.
commonMistakes.right: Please bring your laptop, phone, and charger to the meeting.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence has an unnecessary comma right before 'to the meeting' - once the list is complete, no additional comma is needed before continuing the sentence. Only use commas to separate list items; don't add an extra one right after the last item before continuing the sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: Although, it was raining, we went for a walk.
commonMistakes.right: Although it was raining, we went for a walk.
commonMistakes.why: There shouldn't be a comma right after 'although' - the comma belongs at the end of the entire dependent clause, not right after the conjunction itself. Place the comma after the complete dependent clause ('although it was raining,'), not right after the conjunction word alone.

commonMistakes.wrong: She bought, apples, oranges, and bananas.
commonMistakes.right: She bought apples, oranges, and bananas.
commonMistakes.why: There's an unnecessary comma right after 'bought' - commas should only separate the list items themselves, not come between the verb and the start of the list. Remove any comma that appears between a verb and its object unless there's a specific grammatical reason for it.

commonMistakes.wrong: Its important to, arrive on time.
commonMistakes.right: It's important to arrive on time.
commonMistakes.why: This has two issues: "it's" needs an apostrophe (it is important), and there's an unnecessary comma right before 'arrive' that breaks up the infinitive phrase. Never place a comma between 'to' and the base verb in an infinitive phrase (to arrive, to go, to see).

commonMistakes.wrong: The weather was cold, and, we stayed inside.
commonMistakes.right: The weather was cold, and we stayed inside.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence has an unnecessary extra comma right after 'and' - only one comma is needed before the conjunction joining two complete sentences. Place just one comma before the conjunction (and, but, so) when joining two complete sentences, not an extra one after it too.

commonMistakes.wrong: I like reading; especially mystery novels.
commonMistakes.right: I like reading, especially mystery novels.
commonMistakes.why: A semicolon is too strong here since 'especially mystery novels' isn't a complete sentence on its own - use a comma instead for this kind of added detail. Semicolons should only join two complete, independent sentences; use a comma for incomplete phrases added onto a sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have one goal; to graduate with honors.
commonMistakes.right: I have one goal: to graduate with honors.
commonMistakes.why: A colon (not a semicolon) is used to introduce an explanation, example, or elaboration of what came before - here, the colon correctly introduces what the goal actually is. Remember: colons introduce/explain, semicolons join two complete related sentences - they serve different functions.

commonMistakes.wrong: There are three things I need; milk, eggs, and bread.
commonMistakes.right: There are three things I need: milk, eggs, and bread.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, a colon (not semicolon) correctly introduces this list, since 'milk, eggs, and bread' isn't a complete sentence. Use a colon before a list that explains or elaborates on something mentioned just before it.

commonMistakes.wrong: I finished the report; and I sent it to my manager.
commonMistakes.right: I finished the report, and I sent it to my manager. / I finished the report; I sent it to my manager.
commonMistakes.why: You shouldn't combine a semicolon with a conjunction like 'and' - choose either a comma plus 'and', or a semicolon alone without any conjunction. These are two separate, valid ways to join sentences; don't mix them together.

commonMistakes.wrong: Can I ask you something; it's important.
commonMistakes.right: Can I ask you something? It's important. / Can I ask you something - it's important.
commonMistakes.why: Since the first part is a genuine question, it needs a question mark, not a semicolon, which is reserved for joining two complete statements (not mixing a question with a statement). Match your punctuation to the actual sentence type - questions need question marks, regardless of what follows.

commonMistakes.wrong: the CEO of the company mr. smith, gave a speech.
commonMistakes.right: The CEO of the company, Mr. Smith, gave a speech.
commonMistakes.why: This needs the sentence-starting capital letter, a period after the abbreviation 'Mr.', capitalization of the name 'Smith', and commas around the appositive phrase (Mr. Smith) since it renames 'the CEO'. Titles like Mr., Mrs., and Dr. always need a period after the abbreviation and a capital letter.

commonMistakes.wrong: dr Patel will see you now.
commonMistakes.right: Dr. Patel will see you now.
commonMistakes.why: The title 'Dr' needs both a period (since it's an abbreviation for 'Doctor') and a capital letter, as it's part of the person's name/title. Remember to add periods after common title abbreviations: Dr., Mr., Mrs., Ms.

commonMistakes.wrong: I was born on july 4th 1990.
commonMistakes.right: I was born on July 4th, 1990.
commonMistakes.why: This needs the month capitalized ('July'), and a comma between the day and the year in this date format. Remember the standard date format: Month Day, Year - with a comma separating the day and year.

commonMistakes.wrong: The company's headquarters is in new york city.
commonMistakes.right: The company's headquarters is in New York City.
commonMistakes.why: Every word of this proper place name needs to be capitalized (New York City), following the standard rule for multi-word proper nouns. Treat city and place names the same as other proper nouns - capitalize every significant word in the full name.

commonMistakes.wrong: she works for the united nations.
commonMistakes.right: She works for the United Nations.
commonMistakes.why: Official organization names are proper nouns, so every important word needs a capital letter (United Nations). Capitalize the full official name of organizations, companies, and institutions consistently.

commonMistakes.wrong: Its raining, so bring your umbrella, ok?
commonMistakes.right: It's raining, so bring your umbrella, okay?
commonMistakes.why: This needs the apostrophe fixed (it's), and while 'ok' is common informally, the fuller spelling 'okay' is generally preferred in more careful writing. Use "it's" (not 'its') whenever you mean 'it is', and consider spelling out 'okay' fully in more formal contexts.

commonMistakes.wrong: who's turn is it to cook dinner tonight.
commonMistakes.right: Whose turn is it to cook dinner tonight?
commonMistakes.why: This sentence confuses "who's" (who is) with 'whose' (possession) - since this asks about ownership of the turn, 'whose' is correct, and it also needs a question mark since it's a question. Double-check both the correct word (whose vs who's) and the correct end punctuation for genuine questions.

commonMistakes.wrong: The presentation went well, the client's were impressed.
commonMistakes.right: The presentation went well; the clients were impressed.
commonMistakes.why: This has two fixes: use a semicolon (not comma) to join these two complete, related sentences, and remove the incorrect apostrophe from 'client's' since this is simply a plural (clients), not a possessive. Watch for accidentally adding an apostrophe to a word that's just plural, not showing ownership.

commonMistakes.wrong: we're moving to Chicago in august.
commonMistakes.right: We're moving to Chicago in August.
commonMistakes.why: This needs the sentence-starting capital letter and the month name 'August' capitalized, since months are always proper nouns. Keep checking every sentence for these two very common capitalization spots: the first word, and any months or days mentioned.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: i am going to the market.
I am going to the market.
I Am going to the market.
i Am going to the market.
quiz.answer: I am going to the market.
quiz.explanation: The pronoun 'I' is always capitalized, and sentences start with a capital letter.

quiz.sentence: the meeting is at 3pm on monday
quiz.correction: The meeting is at 3 PM on Monday.
quiz.explanation: Sentence starts and day names (Monday) always need capital letters.

quiz.question: Choose the correctly punctuated possessive:
quiz.explanation: For plural nouns already ending in 's', add just an apostrophe after the s.

quiz.question: ___ (it is) a beautiful day today. (contraction)
quiz.explanation: "It's" is the contraction of 'it is' and needs an apostrophe.

quiz.question: Which punctuation mark introduces a list or explanation?
quiz.explanation: A colon introduces an explanation, example, or list following a complete sentence.
